narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream, and we appreciate the strengths in your profile, like your solid LSAT score of 39, which stands out above the typical student's score. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on academic and background factors, estimated a 72.3% chance that you would not pass it— a threshold that led to the tough decision to reject your application right now. The biggest factor pulling the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, well below the average of around 5.5, signaling to the model that your later academic performance raised concerns about bar exam readiness. Your Year 1 decile of 2, also lower than the typical 5.7, added to this worry by reinforcing a pattern of below-average grades early on. On a more positive note, your LSAT score pushed strongly in the direction of predicting success, countering some of those academic signals effectively. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.90, slightly under the average 3.21, had a smaller but still negative nudge toward failure, while your middle family income bracket played only a minor role in the overall assessment. Being enrolled full-time, like most students, offered a slight positive influence but wasn't enough to shift the balance. Compared to typical applicants, your LSAT is a clear strength that many would envy, but your decile rankings in school years stand out as weaker than average, which weighed heavily here. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Black woman leader in law—it's just the model's data-driven snapshot. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your decile rankings, perhaps seeking targeted tutoring for GPA improvement, and reapplying once you've strengthened those areas, as your LSAT gives you a great foundation. We're rooting for you to turn this around and succeed on your terms.
